question: According to the Revenue Recognition Principle, when should revenue be recognized by a business?
options: When the performance obligation is satisfied and the customer receives control of the promised good or service
When the customer places an order for goods or services
When cash is received from the customer
When the invoice is prepared and sent to the customer
explanation: The Revenue Recognition Principle requires revenue to be recognized when performance obligations are satisfied and control transfers to the customer, not when payment is received. Option C (when cash is received) reflects the older cash basis of accounting, which is incorrect under accrual accounting standards.

question: Which of the following best describes what the Revenue Recognition Principle requires businesses to do?
options: Record revenue in the period when it is earned, regardless of when payment is received
Record revenue only when the company receives payment in full
Record revenue at the end of the fiscal year based on total sales
Record revenue when the accounting department approves the invoice
explanation: The Revenue Recognition Principle is based on accrual accounting, which records revenue when earned (performance obligation satisfied), not when cash arrives. Option B represents cash-basis accounting, which is not compliant with GAAP for most businesses.

question: Apple Inc. sells an iPhone for $1,200 to a customer on December 15, 2024, with payment terms of Net 30. The customer receives the phone immediately. In what period should Apple recognize this revenue?
options: December 2024, when control of the phone transfers to the customer
January 2025, when the customer pays the $1,200
December 2024 and January 2025, split equally
When the customer first uses the phone
explanation: Revenue is recognized in December 2024 when Apple satisfies its performance obligation (delivering the phone) and the customer obtains control, even though payment arrives in January 2025. Option B reflects a cash-basis approach, which violates the Revenue Recognition Principle under accrual accounting.

question: Under the Revenue Recognition Principle, what is the primary condition that must be met before revenue can be recognized?
options: A performance obligation must be satisfied and control of goods/services must transfer to the customer
The seller must physically deliver the goods to the buyer's location
Both parties must sign a written contract
The customer must pay at least 50% of the purchase price
explanation: The core requirement is satisfaction of performance obligations and transfer of control. Delivery is one way control transfers but is not required for all transactions (e.g., digital goods). Option C is too restrictive; many sales occur on verbal orders. Option D adds a payment threshold that has no basis in GAAP.

question: Spotify Premium offers a 12-month subscription for $119.88 paid upfront. When should Spotify recognize the full $119.88 revenue under the Revenue Recognition Principle?
options: Ratably over the 12-month subscription period as the service is delivered each month
Immediately upon receipt of the $119.88 payment
At the end of the 12-month period after all services have been provided
Only when the customer renews the subscription
explanation: Spotify satisfies its performance obligation over time by delivering streaming access each month. Revenue is recognized monthly (approximately $9.99/month) as the service is provided, not all at once when cash is received. Option B is a common misconception—cash receipt triggers cash-basis accounting, not accrual accounting revenue recognition.

question: Microsoft sells software licenses to a corporation for $500,000 on March 1, 2024. The license is immediately activated, but Microsoft must provide 12 months of technical support and software updates as part of the contract. How much revenue should Microsoft recognize on March 1, 2024?
options: A portion of the $500,000 reflecting the portion of total obligations satisfied on March 1 (license only), with the remainder recognized over the 12-month support period
The full $500,000 immediately, since the customer received the license
$500,000 divided by 12 months = $41,667 per month
None until all 12 months of support are completed
explanation: Microsoft must identify separate performance obligations: the license (satisfied at point of sale) and support services (satisfied over time). Revenue is allocated to each obligation and recognized accordingly. Option B incorrectly treats support as non-essential; Option C fails to recognize the immediate satisfaction of the license obligation.

question: A construction company signed a $2,400,000 contract on January 1, 2024, to build a commercial office building. As of December 31, 2024, the company has completed 40% of the work and incurred $900,000 in costs. The customer has paid $950,000 to date. How much revenue should be recognized in 2024 under the Revenue Recognition Principle?
options: $950,000 (amount of cash received)
$900,000 (amount of costs incurred)
$1,200,000 (50% of the contract)
explanation: For performance obligations satisfied over time, revenue is recognized based on progress toward satisfaction (40% completion = 40% of revenue). Option B reflects cash-basis accounting. Option C confuses costs with revenue. Option D is a plausible distractor but uses the wrong percentage of completion.

question: Best Buy sells a laptop for $1,299 with a 3-year extended warranty for $199 (sold together as a bundled contract). The laptop is delivered immediately, and the warranty coverage begins immediately and expires in 3 years. How should Best Buy allocate and recognize revenue?
options: Allocate $1,299 to the laptop (recognize at point of sale) and $199 to the warranty (recognize ratably over 3 years)
Recognize the entire $1,498 at the point of sale since both were sold together
Allocate equally: $749 for the laptop and $749 for the warranty, both recognized over 3 years
Recognize $1,299 immediately and defer the $199 until the first warranty claim is made
explanation: Best Buy must identify two separate performance obligations: delivery of the laptop (satisfied at point of sale) and warranty coverage (satisfied over time). Revenue is allocated proportionally to each and recognized according to when obligations are satisfied. Option B fails to separate performance obligations; Option D incorrectly links recognition to warranty claims rather than service delivery.

question: Netflix recognized $1,200,000 in December 2024 from its Premium subscription service, but some customers had already cancelled their subscriptions in November 2024. The company included their December payments in the December revenue figure. This is:
options: An error; revenue should only be recognized from customers who were active subscribers in December
Correct accounting; all payments received in December should be recorded as December revenue
Correct; the customer paid, so revenue is earned regardless of when they cancel
Appropriate; revenue recognition is based on cash receipts under the Revenue Recognition Principle
explanation: Cancelled customers did not satisfy Netflix's performance obligation in December; their obligation was satisfied (or reversed) in November. Including cancelled subscriptions in December violates the Revenue Recognition Principle. Options B, C, and D all incorrectly treat payment or cash receipt as the trigger for revenue recognition rather than performance obligation satisfaction.

question: A pharmaceutical company signs a 5-year R&D contract with Pfizer worth $5,000,000. Under the contract, the company must perform research activities monthly and deliver monthly reports. Pfizer pays $250,000 quarterly in advance. In Q1 2024, the company receives $750,000 and performs 20% of the annual work. How much revenue should be recognized in Q1 2024?
options: $250,000 based on performance completed in Q1 (20% of annual obligation or 1/5 of total)
$750,000 because that is the cash received in Q1
$1,000,000 based on 5-year contract divided by 5 years, then by 4 quarters
$500,000 as 50% of the advance payment, deferred until remaining work is done
explanation: The company satisfies its performance obligation over time as it completes research monthly. Q1 represents 3 months of a 60-month contract, or 5%, yielding approximately $250,000 in recognized revenue ($5,000,000 × 5%). The fact that $750,000 was received is irrelevant to revenue recognition; this is a common misconception (Option B). Option C miscalculates the timing proportion.

question: A retailer sells gift cards for $10,000 cash on December 15, 2024. By December 31, 2024, customers have redeemed $6,000 of the gift cards. Should the retailer recognize $10,000 or $6,000 as revenue on December 15, 2024?
options: $6,000 only; revenue is recognized as the performance obligation is satisfied (when the retailer delivers goods upon redemption)
$10,000 immediately; the performance obligation is satisfied when the gift card is received
$0 until all gift cards are redeemed
$4,000; the unredeemed balance represents a contingent obligation
explanation: The retailer's performance obligation is not satisfied when the gift card is sold; it is satisfied when the customer redeems the card and receives goods or services. Revenue is recognized as redemptions occur ($6,000 by Dec 31). The $4,000 unredeemed remains a liability (deferred revenue). Option B incorrectly assumes the obligation is satisfied at the sale; Option C and D are overly conservative misapplications.

question: A software company offers a two-year SaaS license for $100,000 with a 30-day money-back guarantee. Under the Revenue Recognition Principle, should revenue be recognized immediately or deferred?
options: Revenue should be recognized immediately upon contract execution, assuming it is probable that the customer will not exercise the return right based on historical experience
Revenue should be fully deferred and recognized only at the end of Year 2 after the return period expires
Revenue should be recognized only when the 30-day period expires
Revenue recognition is suspended until the company receives all cash payments
explanation: Under GAAP, the company should recognize revenue when it is probable the customer will not exercise the return right, which can be estimated using historical data. If return experience shows <5% returns, revenue recognition is appropriate immediately. The 30-day guarantee creates a contract liability for expected returns, but does not prevent revenue recognition. Option B is overly conservative; Option C and D misunderstand the role of guarantees and payment in revenue recognition.

question: Tesla receives a $10,000 down payment on January 15, 2024, for a custom electric vehicle that will be manufactured and delivered on June 30, 2024. The customer cannot cancel after January 31, 2024. On January 15, 2024, Tesla should recognize:
options: No revenue; the performance obligation (delivery of the vehicle) is not satisfied until June 30, 2024, when control transfers
$10,000 as revenue because the customer has committed to the purchase
$0 revenue but a liability of $10,000 for the customer's deposit, with revenue recognized when cash is fully received
A portion of revenue based on manufacturing progress to date
explanation: Tesla has not yet satisfied its performance obligation in January 2024; the vehicle is not delivered and control has not transferred. The $10,000 is a contract liability (customer deposit), not revenue. Revenue is recognized on June 30 when the vehicle is delivered. Option B confuses commitment with performance obligation satisfaction; Option C incorrectly links revenue to full cash receipt.

question: A company enters a 3-year equipment maintenance contract for $300,000 ($100,000 per year). The customer pays $75,000 upfront in Year 1, $100,000 in Year 2, and $125,000 in Year 3. How much revenue should be recognized each year under the Revenue Recognition Principle?
options: $100,000 in each of Years 1, 2, and 3, regardless of payment timing
$75,000 in Year 1, $100,000 in Year 2, and $125,000 in Year 3 (matching cash payments)
$60,000 in Year 1, $100,000 in Year 2, and $140,000 in Year 3 (proportional to cash received)
$0 in Year 1, $150,000 in Year 2, and $150,000 in Year 3 (after break-even)
explanation: The company satisfies its performance obligation evenly over 3 years (maintenance services), so revenue is recognized ratably at $100,000/year. Payment timing is irrelevant under accrual accounting. Option B reflects cash-basis accounting (incorrect). Option C and D use payment amounts to calculate revenue, which violates the Revenue Recognition Principle.
